title: Avoided Stoner instability at a single ordinary Van Hove point
authors: I. S. Tupitsyn, B. Currie, A. V. Chubukov, B. V. Svistunov, E. Kozik, N. V. Prokof'ev
abstract: When the Fermi surface and the Brillouin zone boundary touch at a Van Hove point, mean-field analysis predicts a ferromagnetic (Stoner) instability at finite $T_{MF}$ for any coupling strength due to the divergent density of states. However, the predicted effect has not been observed experimentally. Several qualitative theoretical proposals have been put forward to explain why the mean-field prediction fails. Based on numerically exact results for the two-dimensional Hubbard model with an ordinary Van Hove singularity, we uncover the mechanisms behind the suppression of the ferromagnetic instability. We employ two diagrammatic Monte Carlo approaches: (i) the four-channel self-consistent approximation and (ii) numerically exact method of combinatorial summation of diagrams with controlled resummation of the truncated expansion. We find that the system avoids the Stoner instability down to temperatures an order of magnitude below $T_{MF}$ due to the combination of the downward renormalization of the effective coupling and the suppression of the density of states by the loss of the quasiparticle residue.
summary: This paper explains why the predicted ferromagnetic instability at a Van Hove singularity does not occur in real 2D electron systems. Using advanced diagrammatic Monte Carlo methods, the authors show that the reduction in effective interaction strength and the loss of quasiparticle weight effectively suppress the magnetism. This provides a theoretical resolution to the conflict between mean-field predictions and experimental observations in materials like Sr2RuO4.
main_problem: The paper addresses the discrepancy between mean-field theory, which predicts a ferromagnetic (Stoner) instability at a Van Hove singularity, and experimental/numerical evidence showing that this instability is suppressed.
main_result: The researchers find that the Stoner instability is avoided down to temperatures an order of magnitude below the mean-field prediction due to a combination of downward renormalization of the effective coupling and the suppression of the density of states via loss of quasiparticle residue.
method: The study employs two Diagrammatic Monte Carlo (DiagMC) approaches: the Bold4+ self-consistent approximation and the numerically exact method of Combinatorial Summation (CoS) of Feynman diagrams.
model_or_system: An anisotropic 2D Fermi-Hubbard model on a square lattice with specifically chosen hopping amplitudes to create a flattened Fermi surface and a Van Hove singularity.
key_observables: Static magnetic susceptibility (chi), effective interaction (U*), polarization bubble (Pi), quasiparticle residue (Z), and fermionic self-energy (Sigma).
important_parameters: Interaction strength U = 2t, chemical potential tuned to the Van Hove singularity (mu = mu_VH), and temperature scales relative to the hopping parameter (T/t).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of the diagrammatic expansion and uses Padé/Integral approximants to extrapolate results from finite-order series.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows susceptibility, polarization, and effective coupling using Bold4+; Figure 3 demonstrates the transition from Fermi-liquid to non-Fermi-liquid behavior in the self-energy; Figure 4 presents more controlled CoS results for susceptibility, polarization, and effective coupling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Stoner instability paradox, describes the 2D Hubbard model and the two DiagMC methods used, presents results from the Bold4+ approximation, provides more exact results from the CoS method, and concludes with the physical mechanisms of instability suppression.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Fermi surfaces of the interacting (solid lines) and non-interacting (dashed lines) systems for the same system densities. In the interacting case, at µ = µV H the QV H point is located on the FS (point A); when µ is detuned from µV H by 0.2t, we are dealing with an open FS intersecting BZ boundary at point B. Along y-direction the FS is located far from the BZ boundary (points C and C′ correspond to µ = µvH and µ = µV H+0.2t cases, respectively). Upper inset demonstrates full Fermi surfaces for both non-interacting cases. We show results calculated at T/t = 0.01 when µV H(U, T) = −0.110t (for non-interacting case µV H = 0.76t). Lower inset shows our hopping scheme.

FIG. 2. Bold4+ approximation results for U = 2t at Van Hove singularity. Left panel: static and uniform susceptibility. Middle panel: static polarization bubble diagram at zero momentum computed using dressed Green’s functions; in the low-temperature limit, it saturates to a finite value in contrast with the divergent behavior of Π0 based on the bare Green’s functions (dashed line). Right panel: effective interaction extracted from the magnetic susceptibility and Π using Eq. (2). The black dashed line shows U ∗/U corresponding to Eq. (4).

FIG. 3. Bold4+ results for the frequency dependence of Σ(QV H, ωn) at different temperatures for the cases when the FS touches the BZ boundary at the point A in Fig. 1 (left panel), and when the system is away from Van Hove point and the system has an open FS, which intersects BZ at the point B in Fig. 1 (right panel). Error-bars are within the sym- bol sizes. The dashed line in the right panel is the expected FL behavior Σ(ωn) = −iαωn

FIG. 4. DiagMC-CoS results for U = 2t at Van Hove singularity. Left panel: static and uniform susceptibility (Bold4+ results from Fig. 2 are added for direct comparison). Middle panel: static polarization bubble diagram at zero momentum computed using dressed Green’s functions. It appears to diverge in the low-temperature limit though less dramatically when compared to the of Π0 behavior shown by the dashed line. Right panel: effective interaction extracted from the magnetic susceptibility and Π using Eq. (2). By solid and dashed lines we plot predictions for U ∗/U in Eqs. (1) and Eq. (4).

FIG. 6. Static magnetic susceptibility χ(Q) along the (0, 0) → (π, 0) →(π, π) →(0, 0) path in BZ obtained within the CoS and Bold4+ techniques for U = 2 and T = 0.02 (for Bold4+ data, statistical error bars are within the symbol sizes).
